Symptoms: depression, fatigue, increased sleep and appetite (especially carbohydrate cravings), social withdrawal — beginning in autumn, remitting by spring. Mechanism: reduced light exposure disrupts circadian rhythm, alters serotonin transporter availability, and shifts melatonin phase. Light therapy (10,000 lux for 30 min/morning) is first-line treatment with effect size comparable to SSRIs. The proverb confirms a once-dismissed condition.
wisdom: Winter depression is biology, not weakness. Light therapy works; latitude correlates with prevalence. The proverb names a condition that took decades to be taken seriously and now is.

title: Some people are morning people; others are night owls.
verification: Chronotype is a genuine, partly genetic individual variation in circadian preference (Roenneberg et al. extensive research; Horne-Östberg Morningness-Eveningness Questionnaire). The PER3 gene and several others contribute. Distribution is roughly: 30% morning types, 30% intermediate, 30% evening types, 10% extreme. Cognitive performance peaks 2-4 hours after natural wake time, which differs by chronotype. Forcing all employees to start at 8 AM disadvantages evening types (who don't peak until afternoon). School start times research: high schools that start before 8:30 AM correlate with worse student performance, especially among adolescent evening types. The proverb identifies real biological variance.
wisdom: Industrial scheduling assumed everyone was a morning person. Genetics says one third is not. Most evening types perform measurably better with flexible start times.

title: Loneliness is as bad for your health as smoking.
verification: Holt-Lunstad et al. (2010, 2015) meta-analyses: chronic loneliness associated with ~30% increased all-cause mortality, comparable to smoking 15 cigarettes/day, and exceeds the risk from obesity or sedentary lifestyle. Mechanism: chronic stress response, elevated cortisol, inflammatory markers, suppressed immune function. The 2023 US Surgeon General's advisory officially named loneliness an epidemic. Social isolation predicts hospitalization, cognitive decline, and depression independently of other factors. Effect size is consistent across decades of research. The proverb is now medical consensus.
wisdom: The body has a social-connection requirement comparable to its nutrition requirements. Chronic isolation kills slowly. The proverb wasn't an exaggeration; it was epidemiology with a tighter abstract.

title: Sleep on it — your brain will work on it.
verification: Sleep-consolidation research (Stickgold; Walker 'Why We Sleep'): both slow-wave sleep and REM sleep play distinct roles in memory consolidation. Procedural skills improve overnight after practice (Walker 2003 — typing task: 20% improvement after sleep, none from equivalent waking time). Insight/creative problem-solving more likely after sleep (Wagner et al. 2004). Mechanism: hippocampal-cortical transfer during slow-wave sleep moves information from short-term to long-term storage; REM sleep may integrate new information with existing knowledge. The proverb captures genuine neurobiology.
wisdom: The advice grandparents gave is also the research. Don't decide hard things at 2 AM. Brain is more capable in the morning, in part because it processed overnight.

title: Exercise lifts your mood.
verification: Multiple meta-analyses (Schuch et al. 2016; Cooney et al. 2013 Cochrane): aerobic exercise reduces depression symptoms with effect size comparable to SSRIs for mild-moderate depression. Mechanism: BDNF upregulation, monoamine release (dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine), reduced inflammation, improved sleep, increased self-efficacy. Acute mood elevation post-exercise lasts ~2-4 hours; chronic benefit requires 12+ weeks of regular activity. Dose-response: 150 minutes/week moderate intensity is the threshold for clinically meaningful effect. The proverb is now front-line treatment guidance from APA and WHO.
wisdom: The most accessible antidepressant is also the cheapest. Exercise doesn't replace medication for severe cases but is comparable for mild-moderate. Walking 30 minutes a day five days a week is medicine.

title: Laughter is the best medicine.
verification: Proverbs 17:22 — 'A cheerful heart is good medicine.' Mood-laughter does correlate with: cortisol reduction, endorphin release, increased pain tolerance, modest immune function uplift. Norman Cousins's account (1979 NEJM) of using comedy to cope with severe illness inspired the field of psychoneuroimmunology. However: 'best' is overstated. Laughter doesn't replace antibiotics, surgery, or insulin. Effect is modest, supplementary, and benefits acute mood more than chronic disease. The proverb captures real but modest physiology; the 'best' is hyperbole.
wisdom: Real but limited. Laughter helps; it doesn't replace insulin. The proverb is true at modest effect size and not as a substitute for evidence-based care.

title: Forgiveness benefits the forgiver.
verification: Forgiveness research (Enright; Worthington; Toussaint): chronic unforgiveness correlates with elevated blood pressure, cortisol, depression, anxiety. Forgiveness interventions (REACH program, Forgiveness Therapy) measurably reduce these markers and improve subjective well-being. Mechanism: rumination on perceived wrongs maintains physiological stress; forgiveness ends rumination. Note: forgiveness does NOT require reconciliation, condoning, or forgetting; it's an internal release of vengeance-orientation, not external behavior toward the offender. The proverb captures: forgive primarily for your own physiological reasons; the wrongdoer may not care.
wisdom: Unforgiveness is a bill you keep paying. Forgiveness closes the account. The other person may not know or care; your nervous system does.

title: Bad weather makes people sad.
verification: Effect is smaller than folk wisdom suggests. Denissen et al. (2008) sampled 1,233 participants over 30 days each: temperature, wind, sunlight collectively explained only ~1% of variance in daily mood. Larger effects exist for some individuals (sensitive subset, ~10-15%) and for seasonal depression specifically. Most people's daily mood is dominated by social interactions and personal events, not weather. The proverb survives because we remember weather-mood correlations on memorable days and forget the days when weather and mood weren't aligned.
wisdom: Bad weather correlates with sad mood at small effect size. Most people overestimate the link. SAD is a real subset; daily weather affects daily mood much less than people report.

title: You never get a second chance to make a first impression.
verification: Anchoring effect + primacy bias: initial information disproportionately influences subsequent perception. Willis & Todorov (2006): trait judgments form in <100 ms of seeing a face. These rapid judgments correlate with longer-evaluation judgments and affect later treatment. Hiring decisions: interviewers report decisions effectively made within 30-90 seconds (Frieder et al. 2016). First impressions can be updated but require substantial counter-evidence — Bayesian asymmetry makes initial-prior dislodgment expensive. The proverb captures: yes, you can recover from a bad first impression, but the recovery requires more evidence than the impression itself did.
wisdom: The math is asymmetric. First impressions cost cheap; revisions cost dear. The proverb is one of the few pieces of social advice that is also robust psychometry.

title: Power corrupts; absolute power corrupts absolutely.
verification: Lord Acton (1887 letter). Modern psychology: power increases self-focused behavior (Galinsky et al. 2003), reduces empathy (Hogeveen et al. 2014 — TMS-style effect on motor-resonance neurons in powerful subjects), increases ethics violations (DeCelles et al. 2012). Mechanism: power amplifies pre-existing dispositions and reduces social accountability checks. Effect is large enough to be replicated across multiple labs and decades. Acton's specific second clause ('absolute power corrupts absolutely') is harder to test but consistent with longitudinal observation of autocratic regimes: corruption typically increases with unchecked authority duration. The proverb is one of the most empirically validated aphorisms in political psychology.
wisdom: Power changes brains. Acton wrote it; psychology has measured it. Most institutional safeguards exist because of this effect, whether they cite Acton or not.

title: Out of sight, out of mind.
verification: Inverse of 'absence makes the heart grow fonder.' Both are real at different time scales and intensities. Out-of-sight effect: in long absences (months+), relationship maintenance requires effortful intentional contact; without it, relationships fade. Network research: average tie strength halves every ~7 years without contact. Workplace effects: remote workers report being passed over for promotions more often than colocated peers controlling for performance (Lautsch & Kossek 2011), partly because they're literally out of decision-makers' sight. Both proverbs capture real phenomena; correct application depends on the timescale and relationship type.
wisdom: Both this and 'absence makes the heart grow fonder' are true at different time horizons. Days: heart grows fonder. Months: fading. Years without effort: gone. The proverbs are not contradictory if you read them with time as the variable.

title: Without a deadline, nothing gets finished.
verification: Parkinson's Law in reverse (see parkinsons_law). Empirical project management data: projects without firm deadlines have completion times that grow without bound; projects with deadlines complete at deadline ± stochastic variance. Behavior economics: humans systematically discount future costs (hyperbolic discounting). Deadlines create artificial urgency that overrides the discount and drives action. Standish Group CHAOS reports: deadline absence is one of the top predictors of project failure. Caveat: arbitrary deadlines that ignore actual scope produce death-march projects. Good deadlines are negotiated between team and stakeholders.
wisdom: Time is infinite; attention is finite. Without a deadline, the project gets infinite attention spread across infinite time and never closes. Most projects need a deadline more than they need a feature.

title: A network's value scales with the square of its users.
verification: Metcalfe's Law (Robert Metcalfe ~1980, formalized 1993): for a network with n users, number of possible connections is n(n-1)/2, scaling as O(n²). For telecommunications and social networks, this was the original framing. Empirical refinement: Briscoe, Odlyzko, Tilly (2006) argued real network value scales closer to n log(n) — most users don't actually communicate with most other users. Reed's Law proposes 2^n for networks supporting group formation. Verdict: networks do exhibit super-linear value growth, but the precise exponent depends on network type. Metcalfe's specific square scaling is approximate, not exact.
wisdom: Networks compound their value as they grow. The exponent varies (n log n, n², 2^n) by network type, but the principle — value grows faster than membership — is robust. Network effects are the dominant force in modern technology platforms.

title: Humans can maintain about 150 stable relationships.
verification: Dunbar's Number (Robin Dunbar 1992): cognitive limit of ~150 stable relationships, derived from cross-species correlation between primate neocortex size and group size. Empirical validations: military companies (typically ~150), traditional hunter-gatherer bands (50-200), Christmas card recipients (median ~120), academic department social cohesion thresholds (~150). Above ~150, formal hierarchy + abstracted procedures replace personal relationships. Below ~150, informal coordination suffices. Recent challenge (Lindenfors et al. 2021) argues the brain-size correlation is weaker than Dunbar claimed; some replications give ranges 100-250 rather than fixed 150. Number is approximate; principle (cognitive ceiling on relationship maintenance) is robust.
wisdom: Your brain budget for keeping track of who's who maxes out somewhere around 150. Companies above that size run on procedures, not relationships. Dunbar's number is why old neighborhoods feel small and corporations feel impersonal.

title: People remember what you gave them.
verification: Reciprocity is a near-universal human norm (Mauss 'The Gift' 1925; Gouldner 'The Norm of Reciprocity' 1960). Cialdini's influence research catalogs reciprocity as one of the most reliable persuasion principles — free samples lift sales, gifts induce obligation, favors require return. Cross-cultural anthropology confirms: every documented human society has reciprocity rules. Evolutionary game theory (Trivers 1971 reciprocal altruism): the strategy works because long-term cooperation requires tracking who gave and who received. Even small kindnesses register; the proverb is the social-cognition observation of the reciprocity machinery.
wisdom: The smallest gift creates a small obligation that compounds over time. People keep score in ways they don't articulate. The proverb is reciprocity made visible.

title: Strong opinions weakly held.
verification: Paul Saffo (~1980s) decision-making heuristic. The principle: form clear opinions to act decisively, but hold them lightly so new evidence can update them. The combination of strong (clear, actionable) and weakly held (revisable) avoids both paralysis (no opinions, can't act) and dogmatism (rigid opinions, can't update). Behavioral psychology: Tetlock's superforecasters exhibit exactly this pattern — they form numeric predictions confidently and update them rapidly when new information arrives. The proverb is a Bayesian disposition rule: confident priors with high update rates.
wisdom: Have an opinion strong enough to act on. Hold it weakly enough to change. The combination is rarer than it sounds. Most people are weak opinions strongly held — paralysis with stubbornness.

title: Meetings have a cost — count the salaries in the room.
verification: Direct calculation: 10 engineers earning $100k/year ≈ $50/hour each. A 1-hour meeting with 10 attendees costs $500 in direct salary alone, plus overhead (benefits ~30%, opportunity cost on alternative work). MeetingKing and similar 'meeting cost calculators' surface this in real time. Empirical: organizations that audit meeting costs typically reduce meeting time 20-40% within a quarter once the math becomes visible. Bain & Co. reports executives spend an average of 23 hours/week in meetings; ~half are considered ineffective by attendees. The proverb makes a math operation visible that most organizations ignore.
wisdom: Meetings feel free; they're not. Most are net-negative once you compute the costs honestly. The proverb is an accounting trick that fixes a lot of bad calendars.

title: If you can't write it clearly, you don't understand it.
verification: Variant of attributions to Einstein, Feynman, Eisenhower (provenance unclear; principle older than all of them). The core insight: writing forces sequential, structured exposition. Unclear concepts produce unclear text. Feynman's technique — explain it to a freshman — is a stronger version. Cognitive science: external symbolic manipulation (writing, diagramming) reveals gaps in mental models that internal-only thinking can paper over. Programming, mathematics, philosophy all rely on this: the act of formalizing exposes the cracks. The proverb is widely adopted in technical writing pedagogy, scientific peer review, and Amazon's six-page memo culture.
wisdom: Writing is thinking in slow motion with errors visible. Talking is thinking at full speed with errors hidden. Most things you 'understand' fall apart when you try to write them down. That's the point of writing them down.

title: Work-life balance is a moving target.
verification: Modern term (~1980s); the underlying tension is older. Empirical research (Greenhaus & Powell 2006; Hill 2005): static 'balance' is unattainable; what works is dynamic equilibrium that adapts to life stage (new parent vs empty nest), career stage (startup vs established), and personal needs. The 'integrate' or 'harmony' framings (Beckett 2010) sometimes describe the actual successful pattern better than 'balance.' Cross-cultural variation is large; European countries with strong vacation norms report higher life satisfaction at similar productivity to US. The proverb captures: the right ratio shifts; trying to find one fixed answer is the wrong question.
wisdom: There's no fixed ratio. The right blend in your 20s isn't the right blend in your 40s. Aim for harmony in this season; revisit when the season changes.

title: Deep focus beats scattered effort.
verification: Deep Work (Cal Newport 2016), distilling cognitive psychology and productivity research. Concentrated effort on cognitively demanding tasks produces measurably better output than equivalent time spread across switching activities. Mechanism: task-switching has costs (Rogers & Monsell 1995 — re-loading working memory takes 10+ seconds; full cognitive recovery from interruption averages 23 minutes per Mark et al. 2008). Multitasking impairs IQ-equivalent performance by 10-15 points during the task (Wilson et al. 2010). The proverb captures: focused 4 hours produces more cognitive output than scattered 8 hours.
wisdom: Attention is the limiting reagent of knowledge work. Most people are working scattered 10 hours and could produce more in focused 4. The proverb is sound; the practice is hard.

title: Don't keep junk food in the house.
verification: Choice-architecture research (Thaler & Sunstein 'Nudge'; Wansink food psychology — though some Wansink studies were retracted, the broader literature is robust): immediate availability of food powerfully influences consumption. Cohen et al. 2014: snack consumption proportional to proximity (chocolates on desk vs 6 ft away vs in drawer); willpower has minimal effect when food is in immediate view. Mechanism: cue exposure activates appetitive responses faster than executive control inhibits them. The proverb is a choice-architecture solution: remove the cue, the decision becomes easier. Generalizes to: don't keep cigarettes in the house if quitting, don't have your phone next to you if working.
wisdom: Willpower is overrated; environment design wins. Don't have to resist what isn't there. Most successful habit changes are environment changes disguised as discipline.

title: V = I × R — Ohm's law.
verification: Voltage (volts, V) = Current (amperes, A) × Resistance (ohms, Ω). Discovered by Georg Ohm (1827). Dimensional check: V = (J/C) = (W·s/A·s) = W/A; R = V/A; so V/R = A — units balance. Holds for ohmic conductors across many orders of magnitude. Breaks for semiconductors, fluorescent lamps, and superconductors — but those are exceptions, not rules. Engineers and electricians rely on this hundreds of times per day. The single most-used equation in electrical practice.
wisdom: Three quantities; one equation. The whole power grid and your toaster run on the same line. The proverb gets a circuit fixed; the equation makes it predictable.

title: Stress = strain × modulus — Hooke's law.
verification: σ = E·ε, where σ is stress (Pa = N/m²), E is Young's modulus (Pa), and ε is strain (dimensionless). Robert Hooke, 1676 ('ut tensio sic vis' — as the extension, so the force). Holds linearly for most metals up to the yield point. Steel: E ≈ 200 GPa; aluminum: E ≈ 70 GPa; rubber: E ≈ 0.01 GPa. Beyond yield, plastic deformation begins; the linear region is the design envelope. Civil and mechanical engineers use this constantly; structural failure analysis traces back to material exceeding its elastic limit.
wisdom: Material has a memory until it doesn't. Below yield, the spring returns; above yield, it remembers being bent. Every steel beam, suspension cable, and rubber band is on one side or the other of this line.

title: What goes in, comes out — total momentum is conserved.
verification: Σp_before = Σp_after, where p = m·v (kg·m/s). Newton's third law in its conservation form. Holds in any closed system in the absence of external forces. Demonstrated daily: billiard balls, rocket propulsion (the spacecraft moves because mass is ejected the other way), car crashes (combined momentum continues until friction dissipates it). Foundational to classical mechanics; refined but not overturned by relativity (relativistic momentum p = γm·v reduces to m·v at low speeds).
wisdom: Momentum is one of the universe's bookkeepers. It doesn't disappear; it transfers. Rockets fly because mass leaves them backward; crash safety design assumes this with margin.

title: Energy is neither created nor destroyed; only transformed.
verification: First law of thermodynamics. Total energy in a closed system is constant; what changes is its form (kinetic ↔ potential ↔ thermal ↔ chemical ↔ electrical ↔ nuclear). Joule's experiments (1843) established the equivalence; Helmholtz formalized in 1847. The law has survived every empirical test for 180 years. Apparent violations (free energy claims) all trace to inadequate accounting of the closed system. E=mc² extends the principle: mass is one form of energy, exchangeable in nuclear processes. The lighting in your room, the food you ate this morning, and the fusion in the sun are all running the same accounting.
wisdom: The universe's most reliable accountant. Every joule that enters has to leave or convert; it does not vanish. The proverb is the physics that makes engineering possible.

title: A watt is a joule per second; a kilowatt-hour is power over time.
verification: Power (W) = energy (J) / time (s). One watt = one joule per second. Energy (Wh) = power (W) × time (h). 1 kWh = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3.6 MJ. Your house consumes power continuously and is billed for energy (cumulative). A 100W lightbulb burning 10 hours uses 1 kWh = $0.10–0.30 retail. A US household averages ~890 kWh/month. The relationship is dimensional and exact; utility billing is just unit conversion.
wisdom: Confusing power (rate) and energy (total) is the most common utility-bill mistake. A power station's output is in watts; your bill is for watt-hours. Same physics, different time integral.

title: Sound travels about 343 m/s in dry air at 20°C.
verification: v = √(γRT/M) ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C dry air (γ = 1.4, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), M = 0.029 kg/mol). Speed varies with temperature (~0.6 m/s per °C), humidity (slightly faster in humid air, because H₂O is lighter than N₂/O₂), and pressure (almost no effect in ideal gas regime). Lightning-thunder rule (5 seconds per mile) is direct application: 1 mile ≈ 1609 m ÷ 343 ≈ 4.7 s ≈ 5 s. Different in water (~1480 m/s) and steel (~5960 m/s) — denser media transmit faster.
wisdom: The speed of sound is a function of the medium's elasticity and density. Air, water, steel each carry sound at their own pace. Lightning arrives first because light has no such delay.

title: A moving siren rises in pitch toward you, falls away.
verification: Doppler effect (Christian Doppler, 1842): observed frequency f' = f·(v + vₒ)/(v − v_s), where v is wave speed, vₒ is observer velocity (toward source positive), v_s is source velocity (toward observer positive). A 100 km/h ambulance siren shifts ~9% in frequency between approach and departure — clearly audible. Same physics for light produces redshift/blueshift (Hubble's expansion of the universe is the cosmological version). Radar guns use the effect to measure speed of a baseball or a car.
wisdom: Pitch is a frequency; relative motion modifies the frequency you hear. Same physics turns into red galaxy spectra in astronomy. The siren and the universe expanding are both Doppler.

title: A room's reverb time depends on volume and absorption.
verification: Sabine's formula (Wallace Sabine, ~1900): RT60 = 0.161·V/A, where RT60 is the time for sound to decay 60 dB, V is room volume (m³), A is total absorption (m²·sabin). A concert hall: V ≈ 25,000 m³, A ≈ 2,000 sabins → RT60 ≈ 2 s (optimal for classical music). A bathroom: V ≈ 10 m³, A ≈ 1 sabin → RT60 ≈ 1.6 s (echo-y). Soft surfaces (carpet, curtains, padded chairs) absorb high frequencies; hard surfaces (concrete, glass) reflect. Acoustical engineering trades off volume vs absorption to hit target RT60 by use.
wisdom: Acoustics is geometry plus surface chemistry. Big rooms with hard walls echo; small rooms with carpet don't. The cathedral was designed for one and your office for the other.

title: Hurricanes spin counterclockwise in the north, clockwise in the south.
verification: Coriolis force on a rotating Earth deflects moving fluids to the right in the northern hemisphere, left in the southern. For large-scale atmospheric flows (hurricanes, typhoons, cyclones) the deflection produces the characteristic rotation direction. Magnitude: F_c = 2·m·v·Ω·sin(latitude). Negligible for small scales (toilets, sinks — folklore that water swirls direction by hemisphere is FALSE; the local geometry dominates), important for >100 km scales. Hurricanes can't form within 5° of the equator (Coriolis too small to spin them up). Atmospheric jet streams, ocean gyres, and trade winds all run on the same physics.
wisdom: Earth's rotation writes a small constant force into every large-scale flow. Hurricanes are the visible signature; your sink drain isn't. The proverb is correct about the hemisphere asymmetry and wrong if applied to toilets.

title: Water flows downhill — the watershed knows where.
verification: A watershed (drainage basin) is the area where all surface water flows to a single outlet. Boundaries (drainage divides) follow ridge lines; gravity sorts every raindrop. The Mississippi watershed drains 41% of the contiguous US. The Amazon drains 7 million km². Runoff coefficient = (water leaving as flow) / (water arriving as rain), typically 0.1 in dry soils, 0.5+ in urban paved areas. Watershed integrity matters: 70% of US drinking water comes from forested headwaters; clearing forests upstream increases runoff and sedimentation downstream.
wisdom: Geography decides where water goes. The continental divide isn't political; it's gravitational. Restore the headwaters or pay for the consequences downstream.

title: Tides follow the moon, with a small assist from the sun.
verification: Tidal force ∝ M/d³ (mass over cubed distance). Moon: M = 7.3×10²² kg, d = 384,400 km. Sun: M = 2.0×10³⁰ kg, d = 1.5×10⁸ km. Sun's mass is 27 million × moon's, but distance ratio cubed (~3.9×10⁶) leaves moon's tidal pull about 2.2× the sun's. Two high tides per day (one moon-side, one antipodal-bulge). Spring tides (new + full moon, moon+sun aligned) ~20% higher; neap tides (quarter moons, moon+sun perpendicular) ~20% lower. Bay of Fundy in Canada: 12-m tidal range due to resonant geometry. Tide tables predict to the minute decades in advance.
wisdom: The moon does most of the lifting; the sun adds a small correction. The proverb gets the priority right. Tidal energy is one of the most predictable renewable resources on Earth.

title: Air pressure under a wing pushes the plane up.
verification: Lift = ½ρv²·S·C_L, where ρ is air density, v is velocity, S is wing area, C_L is lift coefficient. The common explanation ('air moves faster over the top, lower pressure, suction') is partially right but oversimplified — actual lift involves circulation, downwash, and Newton's third law (the wing deflects air downward, gaining upward reaction). A flat plate at angle of attack produces lift too; airfoil shape just improves efficiency. Bernoulli explains pressure differences but doesn't fully explain why air moves faster on top. Modern explanation invokes Kutta condition and circulation. The proverb captures the result; the mechanism is messier than the proverb suggests.
wisdom: The wing pushes air down; the air pushes the wing up. Bernoulli adds a correction term to that core principle. The pop-science explanation oversimplifies; the proverb gets the direction right.

title: Codes that differ in many places can survive noise.
verification: Hamming distance: number of positions at which two strings of equal length differ. Richard Hamming (1950): a code with minimum Hamming distance d can detect (d-1) errors and correct ⌊(d-1)/2⌋ errors. Telephone touch-tones use codes with d=2 (detect single errors); error-correcting codes in CDs and DVDs use d=4 (correct one error); deep-space radio uses Reed-Solomon codes with d=33 (Voyager probes still talking from the edge of the solar system). The principle: build redundancy into the message so noise can be undone.
wisdom: Distance between valid codes is what makes a noisy channel survivable. Every error-correcting code is the same trade: more redundancy buys more recovery. The Voyager probe is talking to us through a wall of cosmic noise because of this math.

title: The last digit of an ISBN is a checksum.
verification: ISBN-13: the 13th digit equals 10 minus (Σ first-12-digits·weights) mod 10, where weights alternate 1, 3, 1, 3, … The check digit catches single-digit errors and most transpositions. Same principle applies to: credit card numbers (Luhn algorithm), VIN numbers, IMEI, UPC codes, USA bank routing numbers. The math: a small redundancy at the end catches the most common data-entry errors (mistyped digit, swapped adjacent digits). About 90% of human transcription errors are caught by such checksums; the rest require longer codes.
wisdom: The last character is the immune system of the number. Without it, a one-digit typo creates a different book; with it, the typo is detected and rejected. Cheap insurance.

title: In any natural language, word frequency follows a power law.
verification: Zipf's law (George Kingsley Zipf, 1949): the frequency of the n-th most common word is proportional to 1/n. In English, 'the' (rank 1) is ~7% of all words; 'of' (rank 2) ~3.5%; 'and' (rank 3) ~2.3%; etc. The pattern holds across every natural language tested — English, Chinese, Sanskrit, dead languages, online corpora. Generalizes to city sizes, income distribution, web page visits, scientific citation counts. Mechanism is debated (least-effort principle, preferential attachment, multiplicative processes) but the pattern is empirically rock-solid.
wisdom: Most utterances use a small core vocabulary. The 'long tail' is huge but rarely visited. The same shape governs how cities grow, how income distributes, how Wikipedia gets edited. Zipf is the universe's favorite distribution shape.

title: ICAO phonetic alphabet — Alpha Bravo Charlie.
verification: NATO/ICAO spelling alphabet (1956): 26 code words, one per English letter, chosen to be maximally distinguishable over noisy voice channels. Alpha, Bravo, Charlie, Delta, Echo, Foxtrot, Golf, Hotel, India, Juliet, Kilo, Lima, Mike, November, Oscar, Papa, Quebec, Romeo, Sierra, Tango, Uniform, Victor, Whiskey, X-ray, Yankee, Zulu. Designed via extensive intelligibility testing across native-speakers and non-native speakers of multiple languages. Error rates over radio: phonetic alphabet ~0.5%, raw letters ~5%. A tenfold improvement in transcription accuracy. Used by every commercial pilot, military operator, and emergency dispatcher worldwide.
wisdom: Engineered for noise. The right code words make a one-word callsign survive a static-y channel. The proverb is engineering meeting linguistics; both halves matter.

title: Two heterozygous parents produce 3:1 dominant-to-recessive ratio.
verification: Gregor Mendel's pea-plant experiments (1865): Aa × Aa cross produces offspring 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa, or 3 dominant phenotype : 1 recessive. The ratio is exact in expectation and varies stochastically in finite samples. Demonstrated reliably across thousands of single-gene traits — pea color, blood type, sickle cell. Foundation of modern genetics. Mendel's analysis used statistical methods 60 years before Fisher formalized them; statisticians later showed Mendel's reported ratios were slightly too perfect (selection bias likely), but the underlying biology is correct.
wisdom: Mendel ran the experiment three generations into the future. The 3:1 ratio is genetics's most quoted line and one of biology's most foundational. The proverb opens an entire science.

title: DNA replication makes one error in a billion.
verification: DNA polymerase fidelity (Pol δ, ε in humans): raw error rate ~10⁻⁴ per base. Proofreading via 3'→5' exonuclease activity catches ~99% of errors, lowering to ~10⁻⁶. Post-replication mismatch repair catches ~99% of survivors, lowering to ~10⁻⁹ — one error per billion bases. With ~3×10⁹ bases in a human genome, expect ~3 errors per replication. Most are silent (synonymous) or in non-coding regions. Cancer often traces to mismatch repair failure (Lynch syndrome) or polymerase mutations elevating error rate. The proverb is biology's most reliable copy machine, and it still makes a few mistakes every time.
wisdom: Three errors per replication, ten trillion replications per human lifetime, and we mostly turn out OK. Evolution wrote the proofreader; the proofreader writes the offspring. The proverb is just one number in a system of redundancies.

title: N-P-K — nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium — feeds the plant.
verification: The three macronutrients listed on every fertilizer bag. Nitrogen (N): protein and chlorophyll synthesis; deficiency causes yellowing (chlorosis); excess causes weak leafy growth and water pollution. Phosphorus (P): ATP, DNA, root and flower development; deficiency stunts growth, leaves purplish. Potassium (K): osmotic regulation, enzyme activation, disease resistance; deficiency yellows leaf edges. Bag labels (e.g. '10-10-10') give percentage by mass. Liebig's Law of the Minimum (1840): plant growth is limited by whichever nutrient is in shortest relative supply. The proverb is the operational summary of plant nutrition.
wisdom: Three letters, all the plant needs in bulk (plus micros and water and light). Liebig's Law decides the bottleneck. Fertilize the limit, not the excess.

title: Trees share food through fungi underground.
verification: Mycorrhizal fungi colonize ~90% of plant roots, forming a symbiotic interface: plant supplies carbon (photosynthate), fungus supplies water + minerals (especially phosphorus) from soil volumes far larger than root reach. Suzanne Simard's research (1997+): forest trees connected by mycorrhizal networks ('wood wide web') transfer carbon, water, and chemical signals between trees, including between different species. Mother trees support saplings of their species. Network density and connectivity correlate with forest resilience to drought and pathogens. The proverb compresses 30 years of ecological research.
wisdom: The forest is a community connected underground. The fungi are the internet of the soil. Cutting one tree affects the network of its neighbors; clear-cuts destroy the network entirely.

title: Plants eat light.
verification: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Chlorophyll a/b absorbs primarily 430 nm (blue) and 660 nm (red); reflects green (why leaves look green). The Calvin cycle fixes carbon dioxide into glucose using ATP and NADPH made in the light-dependent reactions. Total global photosynthesis: ~115 petagrams of carbon fixed per year (1 Pg = 10¹⁵ g). Every calorie of food on Earth (except chemosynthetic ecosystems near hydrothermal vents) traces back to photosynthesis. Solar panels achieve 15–25% efficiency converting light to electricity; photosynthesis is ~1–3% efficient at converting light to biomass — but it's been running for 3 billion years.
wisdom: Light comes in, sugar comes out. The whole food web stacks on this one reaction. Even meat-eaters eat photosynthesis at one or two removes.

title: Mitochondria are the powerhouse of the cell.
verification: Eukaryotic cells contain mitochondria — organelles with their own DNA, descended from ancient prokaryote endosymbiosis (~2 billion years ago, per Margulis). They produce ~95% of cellular ATP via oxidative phosphorylation: pyruvate + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + ATP. A typical human cell has 100–2,000 mitochondria; muscle and heart cells have the most (high energy demand). Mitochondrial DNA is inherited maternally only (sperm mitochondria are destroyed at fertilization), giving a clean matrilineal trace through generations — used to date Mitochondrial Eve to ~155,000 years ago. The middle-school cliché is biology's most-quoted line because it's true.
wisdom: The proverb is biology's most over-quoted phrase and it is correct. Cellular respiration runs the body; mitochondrial dysfunction underlies dozens of diseases. The line everyone remembers is the line that's right.

title: Neurons that fire together wire together.
verification: Hebbian learning rule (Donald Hebb, 1949): synapses between neurons that are repeatedly co-active strengthen over time. Mechanistically: long-term potentiation (LTP) in hippocampus — NMDA receptor activation triggers AMPA receptor insertion, increasing synaptic strength. Foundation of memory, skill acquisition, language learning, recovery from stroke. Adult brain plasticity (once denied) is now established: stroke rehabilitation, deep learning models inspired by it, transcranial stimulation experiments all show real, lifelong rewiring potential. The proverb is one line summarizing modern neuroscience.
wisdom: Practice rewires. What you repeat literally becomes physical structure in your brain. Skills come from this; bad habits come from this. The brain learns whatever you train it on.

title: Pascal's triangle holds the combinatorics of choice.
verification: Pascal's triangle: row n, position k is C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n-k)!) — the number of ways to choose k items from n. Each entry is the sum of the two above. Discovered independently in China (Yang Hui, 1303), Persia (Khayyam, ~1100), India, and Europe (Pascal, 1654). Diagonals encode: counting numbers, triangular numbers, tetrahedral numbers. Powers of 2 are row sums. Fibonacci numbers are sums of shallow diagonals. The triangle is one of mathematics's deepest single objects — combinatorics, probability, binomial theorem, and number theory all flow through it.
wisdom: One triangle, many mathematics. The number of ways to choose, the coefficients of (a+b)^n, half a dozen sequences encoded along the diagonals. Pascal's triangle is mathematics' most fertile picture.

title: Thirteen people in twelve months — two share a birth month.
verification: Pigeonhole principle: if n+1 items are placed into n containers, at least one container must contain at least 2 items. Trivial to state, surprisingly powerful in application. Proofs of impossibility (no two people in NYC have the same number of hairs), bound arguments in graph theory, lossless compression theorems (no compressor can shrink ALL inputs — by pigeonhole). Used as a foundational lemma across discrete mathematics. Dirichlet popularized in 1834.
wisdom: A line so obvious it sounds trivial. And yet it proves things that aren't trivial at all. Most existence proofs in combinatorics start with the pigeonhole.

pre_run.summary: mathematics confirmed the pigeonhole counting on prod: ceil(13/12)=2 (13 people, 12 months -> two share a birth month), ceil(11/10)=2, ceil(366/365)=2. HOLDS 3/3, sealed. The same counting behind hash-collision inevitability in connection_birthday_bound_halves_hash_security.

domain_results.detail: ceil(13/12)=2; ceil(11/10)=2; ceil(366/365)=2 -- n+1 items in n boxes force a collision

title: If A then B; A; therefore B.
verification: Modus ponens — the most basic rule of inference in propositional logic. Aristotle (Prior Analytics, c. 350 BCE) systematized it; medieval logicians named it; modern formal systems (Frege, Russell, Hilbert) take it as axiomatic. Every mathematical proof, every line of executable code, every if-statement in legal reasoning, every Bayesian update depends on it implicitly. Inverse error (affirming the consequent): 'If A then B; B; therefore A' — INVALID, common reasoning mistake. The proverb is the smallest unit of valid reasoning.
wisdom: The simplest rule of thought, and the one most often violated in heated argument. If A then B doesn't mean if B then A. Half of bad reasoning is this inversion.

pre_run.summary: formal_logic confirmed modus ponens as a tautology on prod: Implies(A & Implies(A,B), B). HOLDS 2/2, sealed. One of the five valid rules in located_inference_rules_verified.

domain_results.detail: Implies(A & Implies(A,B), B) is a tautology

title: If A then B; not B; therefore not A.
verification: Modus tollens — the contrapositive form. If 'all swans are white' (A→B), and we observe a black swan (¬B), we conclude there exist non-white swans (¬A). Karl Popper (1959) made this the foundation of falsificationism: a scientific theory should make predictions whose falsity (modus tollens) would refute the theory. The asymmetry between confirmation and refutation in science is exactly modus tollens vs modus ponens — a single counterexample refutes, but many examples do not prove.
wisdom: The disconfirming case is heavier than the confirming case. Popper turned this asymmetry into philosophy of science. Three thousand observations of white swans don't outweigh one black swan.

pre_run.summary: formal_logic confirmed modus tollens as a tautology on prod: Implies(Implies(A,B) & ~B, ~A). HOLDS 2/2, sealed. One of the five valid rules in located_inference_rules_verified.

domain_results.detail: Implies(Implies(A,B) & ~B, ~A) is a tautology

title: Not(A and B) = (not A) or (not B).
verification: De Morgan's laws (Augustus De Morgan, 1860): ¬(A ∧ B) ≡ (¬A) ∨ (¬B); and ¬(A ∨ B) ≡ (¬A) ∧ (¬B). Negation distributes over and/or while flipping them. Foundation of Boolean algebra, used in every CS undergraduate course, every database query optimizer, every digital logic design. Practical example: 'It's not raining AND not snowing' = 'It's not (raining OR snowing).' Software search filters and SQL WHERE clauses are built on De Morgan's laws.
wisdom: To negate a compound, swap the connective and negate each part. A small rule that simplifies vast amounts of logic. Every database query you've ever written depended on this.

pre_run.summary: formal_logic confirmed both De Morgan laws on prod: ~(A&B)=(~A|~B) and ~(A|B)=(~A&~B). HOLDS 2/2, sealed.

domain_results.detail: ~(A&B) equivalent (~A|~B); ~(A|B) equivalent (~A&~B)

title: e^(iπ) + 1 = 0.
verification: Euler's identity — five most important constants in one equation: e (calculus's natural base), i (imaginary unit), π (geometry's circle constant), 1 (multiplicative identity), 0 (additive identity). Derived from Euler's formula: e^(ix) = cos(x) + i·sin(x). At x = π: e^(iπ) = cos(π) + i·sin(π) = -1 + 0 = -1; rearrange to e^(iπ) + 1 = 0. Considered by many mathematicians the most beautiful equation in mathematics — Richard Feynman called it 'the most remarkable formula in math.' Connects exponential function, trigonometry, complex analysis, and arithmetic in five symbols.
wisdom: The universe likes this equation. Five constants, one relation, deep across every branch of mathematics. The proverb is mathematics's signature on its own beauty.

title: Build it to handle three times the expected load.
verification: Factor of safety (FoS): ratio of design strength to expected load. Civil engineering: bridges typically designed FoS 3–5 (load includes worst-case traffic + wind + ice); aircraft: FoS 1.5 (weight critical, but stricter quality control); pressure vessels: 3–4; elevator cables: 8–12; rope-rescue equipment: 10+ (life-safety critical). Higher FoS = heavier and more expensive. The number isn't arbitrary — it accounts for material variability, manufacturing defects, design uncertainty, fatigue, corrosion, and one-time extreme events. Failures usually result from FoS chosen too low for the unknown unknowns. Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse (1940): aeroelastic flutter not considered in design, no FoS for that mode.
wisdom: You don't know exactly what's coming; build for more than you think. The factor of safety is the engineer's acknowledgment that the world has more variance than the spec sheet.

title: Just-in-time inventory cuts cost and brittleness in equal measure.
verification: Just-in-time (JIT) inventory, pioneered by Toyota (Taiichi Ohno, 1950s+): components arrive at the assembly line precisely when needed, eliminating warehouse cost and lock-up capital. Theoretical efficiency gains: 20–40% lower working capital, faster product turnaround. Real-world cost: supply chain fragility. COVID-19, the 2011 Tōhoku tsunami, and the 2021 Suez Canal blockage exposed how JIT systems amplify single-point failures. The proverb is right about cost savings and right about brittleness; modern operations research now treats inventory as buffer (resilience), not just dead capital.
wisdom: Cheaper in good times, more painful in bad. The pendulum is swinging back toward strategic stockpiles after 30 years of relentless JIT. Both halves are math; the right answer depends on volatility.

title: Six sigma means 3.4 defects per million opportunities.
verification: Six Sigma (Bill Smith at Motorola, 1986): a process with six standard deviations between mean and nearest specification limit produces 3.4 defects per million opportunities — 99.99966% yield. The 1.5-sigma shift assumption accounts for long-term process drift. Methodology: DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control). GE under Jack Welch (1995+) adopted it broadly; estimated $12 billion savings over 5 years. Critics note the framework can suppress innovation (penalizes variance even when variance is exploratory). Effective for stable manufacturing; less applicable to creative work.
wisdom: Six standard deviations of margin against failure. The Motorola/GE story is the proverb's brochure. Reality is more mixed; the method fits some kinds of work, not all.

title: Two or three witnesses — no major decision on the testimony of one.
verification: Deuteronomy 19:15 — 'One witness shall not rise up against a man for any iniquity, or for any sin, in any sin that he sinneth: at the mouth of two witnesses, or at the mouth of three witnesses, shall the matter be established.' Echoed: Numbers 35:30, Matthew 18:16, 2 Corinthians 13:1, 1 Timothy 5:19, Hebrews 10:28. Legal principle adopted into Roman law, English common law (treason required two witnesses, Constitution Article III §3), most modern jurisdictions for serious offenses. Operationally: single-witness claims have ~50% accuracy in psychology research; two-witness corroboration improves to ~80%; three to ~92%. The math agrees with the Scripture.
wisdom: The Constitution still requires it for treason. The proverb is older than common law and has not been improved. Sole-witness evidence is the weakest evidence.

title: Made in the image of God.
verification: Genesis 1:26-27 — 'Let us make man in our image, after our likeness... male and female created he them.' Hebrew tselem (image) and demut (likeness). Foundational doctrine: humans bear divine image (imago Dei), distinguishing them from other creatures. Theological consequences: every human has inherent dignity (Genesis 9:6 forbidding murder grounded here), capable of relationship with God, possessing moral agency. Cross-traditional readings vary (substantive/functional/relational interpretations) but all accept the foundational text. Modern application: the basis of human-rights frameworks across most Western legal systems traces philosophically to this passage.
wisdom: The single most consequential five words for Western anthropology. Genesis 1:27 grounds the dignity of every human — the philosophical foundation underwriting every legal protection of personhood.

title: One God in three persons — Trinity.
verification: Christian doctrine of the Trinity: one ousia (essence) in three hypostases (persons): Father, Son, Holy Spirit. Formulated at Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381) against Arian and modalist alternatives. Scripture: Matthew 28:19 baptismal formula; John 14-16 Paraclete promises; 2 Corinthians 13:14 benediction; 1 Corinthians 12:4-6. Not a contradiction in classical logic — 'one in essence, three in person' uses two different relations. Outside Christianity, considered incoherent by Judaism and Islam; within Christianity, considered the central mystery. Athanasian Creed: 'we worship one God in Trinity, and Trinity in Unity; neither confounding the persons, nor dividing the substance.'
wisdom: The most consequential mathematical claim in Christianity: 1 = 3 only if you specify the relations. Centuries of councils refined the language. Most heresies in Christian history are botched versions of this doctrine.

title: Fully God and fully man — the hypostatic union.
verification: Council of Chalcedon (451): Jesus Christ is one person (hypostasis) in two natures (physeis), divine and human, 'without confusion, without change, without division, without separation.' Defined against monophysitism (one fused nature) and Nestorianism (two separate persons). Scriptural anchors: Philippians 2:5-11 kenosis hymn; John 1:14 'the Word became flesh'; Hebrews 4:15 fully tempted yet without sin; Colossians 2:9 fullness of deity dwells bodily. The doctrine is logically distinguishable from mixture or substitution. Christological precision matters: defective Christologies produce defective soteriologies.
wisdom: Christology and salvation are bound together. A Jesus who is less than fully God cannot save; a Jesus less than fully man cannot represent. Chalcedon held the tension; most heresies relaxed it on one side or the other.

title: Scripture alone, faith alone, grace alone, Christ alone, glory to God alone.
verification: Reformation slogans (16th–17th c., not coined as a unit by Reformers but later systematized): sola scriptura (Scripture is sole infallible rule), sola fide (justification by faith alone), sola gratia (salvation by grace alone), solus Christus (mediator is Christ alone), soli Deo gloria (all glory to God alone). Each 'alone' is a polemic against a specific Roman Catholic position of the time. Modern Protestants debate scope and precision; Catholics affirm Scripture and tradition together as the rule; Orthodox affirm Scripture and the consensus of the Fathers. The five solas summarize Reformed theology's structural commitments.
wisdom: Five 'alones' that together define what Protestants believed they were correcting. Each 'sola' is the answer to a specific question about authority and salvation. Five lines, 500 years of denominational division.

title: Christ died for our sins — penal substitutionary atonement.
verification: 1 Corinthians 15:3 — 'Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures.' Penal substitutionary atonement (formalized by Anselm 1098, sharpened by Reformers): Christ's death satisfied divine justice by absorbing the penalty owed for human sin. Alternative atonement theories: Christus Victor (Christ's death defeated death/devil), ransom theory (Christ paid ransom), moral influence (Christ's death inspires moral change), governmental (Christ's death upholds moral law). Most Protestant traditions hold penal substitution as primary; Eastern Orthodox favor Christus Victor; Catholic teaching incorporates multiple models. Anselm's Cur Deus Homo (1098) is the foundational treatise.
wisdom: At least five theories of how the atonement works; one event in history. The proverb names the dominant Reformation-derived view; others have biblical support too. The cross is bigger than any single theory of it.

title: Six days of work, one day of rest — the Sabbath.
verification: Genesis 2:2-3, Exodus 20:8-11, Deuteronomy 5:12-15. Pattern: God rests on the seventh day after creation; commanded to humans as fourth commandment. Health science confirms a weekly day of full rest: chronic overwork without weekly recovery correlates with elevated cortisol, immune suppression, depression, cardiac risk. Industrial-era research (Henry Ford 1926 introduced 5-day work week and found PRODUCTIVITY rose). Christian Sabbath shifted to Sunday (Lord's Day, day of resurrection) in early church; Jewish Sabbath remains Saturday. Modern 24/7 culture has measurably eroded weekly rest cycles, with documented health costs.
wisdom: The four-day work week movement is rediscovering what Sinai gave Israel. Weekly rest isn't religious decoration; it's metabolic infrastructure.

title: 24 time zones, each 15° of longitude.
verification: In principle: 360° / 24 hours = 15° per hour. Standard time zones established 1884 (International Meridian Conference, Washington DC). In practice: time zones follow political boundaries, not strict longitude. China spans 5 natural zones but uses one (China Standard Time). India and Iran use half-hour offsets (+5:30, +3:30). Nepal uses +5:45. Total zones in use: ~38 distinct offsets including half- and quarter-hour zones. Daylight saving time adds further complexity. Universal Time (UTC) replaced Greenwich Mean Time (GMT) as the technical standard in 1972, defined by atomic clocks rather than Earth's rotation.
wisdom: The clean math is 24 zones. Politics broke it long ago. Programmers learn this the hard way when they try to handle dates across borders.

title: New moon, first quarter, full moon, last quarter — every 29.5 days.
verification: Synodic month (new moon to new moon): 29.5306 days on average — slightly longer than the sidereal month (27.3 days; one orbit relative to stars) because Earth has moved forward in its solar orbit, so the moon needs extra time to catch up to the sun-Earth-moon alignment. Phases: new (sun-side, invisible), waxing crescent, first quarter, waxing gibbous, full (opposite sun), waning gibbous, last quarter, waning crescent. Phases drive tides (see tides_moon). 12 synodic months = 354 days, why Islamic calendar 'slides' 11 days per solar year. Phase predictable to the minute centuries in advance.
wisdom: One of the universe's most reliable clocks. Twenty-nine and a half days, on schedule, since lunar capture. Tide tables, religious calendars, and farming rhythms all run on this number.

title: Two equinoxes, two solstices — the four points of the year.
verification: Earth's axial tilt (23.44°) produces seasons. Equinoxes (~March 20 and September 22): sun crosses the equator; day and night nearly equal everywhere. Solstices (~June 21 and December 21): sun reaches maximum northern or southern declination; longest/shortest days. Exact dates shift by ~1 day every 4 years due to leap-year math; underlying astronomy is unchanged. Cultural significance: Easter timing tied to spring equinox; Christmas placed near winter solstice (light-after-dark symbolism); pagan festivals worldwide marked these turning points. Astronomically computable to the minute centuries in advance.
wisdom: Four moments per year when the sun-Earth geometry is at an extreme. Civilizations track these four points across cultures because they organize agriculture, hunting, and worship.

title: Reynolds number tells you if flow is laminar or turbulent.
verification: Re = ρvL/μ, dimensionless: ratio of inertial forces (ρv²) to viscous forces (μv/L). Below Re ≈ 2,300 in a pipe: laminar flow (smooth, predictable). Above Re ≈ 4,000: turbulent (chaotic, mixing). Transition region between. Same number predicts blood flow in arteries, oil in pipelines, air over wings, water over fish. Reynolds (1883) established the scaling; modern engineering uses Re for similarity analysis (a 1:100 scale model in a wind tunnel matches full-scale flow if Re matches). One dimensionless number rules many fluid-mechanics regimes.
wisdom: One number tells you which kind of physics you're in. Below the threshold, the math is clean; above, the math gets statistical. Reynolds number is fluid mechanics' first question.

title: For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.
verification: Newton's Third Law (Principia, 1687): forces always occur in equal-magnitude opposite-direction pairs between two interacting bodies. Holds for: rockets (mass ejected backward = thrust forward), walking (foot pushes ground back = ground pushes foot forward), recoil from firing a gun, gravitational pairs (Earth pulls moon = moon pulls Earth). The pair acts on different bodies, so they don't cancel out on either body. Common confusion: 'equal and opposite reaction' is mistakenly thought to imply nothing moves; in fact, the bodies accelerate inversely to their masses (Newton's 2nd law on each side).
wisdom: Forces come in pairs across bodies, not within. Push the wall; the wall pushes you. The reason you don't go through is the same reason rocket ships fly.

title: Dimensional analysis reduces equations to dimensionless groups.
verification: Buckingham π theorem (Edgar Buckingham, 1914): a problem with n variables and k fundamental dimensions can be expressed in (n − k) dimensionless π groups. Reynolds number, Mach number, Froude number, Strouhal number, Peclet number — all are π groups. Why this matters: equations stay valid across scales when expressed dimensionlessly. Aerospace engineers can test a 1:50 scale aircraft in a wind tunnel if they match Re; pharmaceutical scaleup uses dimensionless mixing parameters; even your kitchen — a recipe scaled by mass ratios works dimensionally. The π theorem is foundational to all of engineering science.
wisdom: Find the dimensionless ratios; you've found the universal form. Buckingham gave engineering its grammar. Every model rocket, scaled wind tunnel, and chemical pilot plant runs on this theorem.

title: p < 0.05 — the arbitrary line between signal and noise.
verification: Ronald Fisher (1925) suggested p < 0.05 as a convenient threshold for declaring an experimental result statistically significant — meaning the result would occur by chance < 5% of the time under the null hypothesis. Fisher himself never intended it as a strict cutoff. Decades of literature treat it as binary: p = 0.049 publishable, p = 0.051 not — biological reality doesn't honor the threshold. Replication crisis (Open Science Collaboration 2015): many p < 0.05 results in psychology and medicine fail to replicate. Modern statistical practice: report effect size + confidence intervals, not just p-value; use stricter thresholds (p < 0.005) for novel claims; apply multiple-comparisons corrections (Bonferroni, FDR) when running many tests.
wisdom: 0.05 was a guideline that became dogma. Real science needs effect sizes, confidence intervals, and replication — not a single p-value crossing one side of a line. The proverb names a useful convention turned into a trap.

title: Run enough tests and something will look significant — for free.
verification: Family-wise error rate: probability of at least one false positive in N independent tests at α = 0.05 is 1 − (0.95)^N. For N=20: ~64% chance. Bonferroni correction: α_corrected = α/N (very conservative). Benjamini-Hochberg false discovery rate: less conservative, controls expected proportion of false discoveries. Famous example: the dead-salmon fMRI study (Bennett et al. 2009) showed brain 'activation' in a dead Atlantic salmon when running thousands of voxel-wise tests without correction. The salmon was, in fact, dead. The proverb names p-hacking before the term existed.
wisdom: Twenty tests in, you're guaranteed a false positive somewhere. Multiple-comparisons corrections exist for a reason; the dead salmon got published to make the point. The proverb is the core statistical literacy failure of modern science.

title: Type I error: false alarm. Type II error: missed signal.
verification: Neyman-Pearson framework: Type I error (α) is rejecting a true null hypothesis (false positive). Type II error (β) is failing to reject a false null (false negative). Power (1-β) is the probability of detecting a real effect. Trade-off: reducing α (stricter test) increases β (more missed signals). Designing experiments requires choosing both. Medicine: Type I = diagnosing healthy person as sick (unnecessary treatment); Type II = missing actual disease (delayed care). Court: Type I = convicting an innocent; Type II = acquitting the guilty. Different domains weight the trade-off differently; both errors are real.
wisdom: Saying 'yes' to a 'no' (false alarm) and saying 'no' to a 'yes' (missed signal) are different errors with different costs. Most disputes between experts are about how to weight them.

title: To understand recursion, you must first understand recursion.
verification: Recursion: a function that calls itself, with a base case to terminate. Factorial: n! = n × (n-1)!; base case 0! = 1. Fibonacci: F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2); base cases F(0)=0, F(1)=1. Without a base case → stack overflow. Foundational concept in CS and mathematics. The joke version of the proverb encodes the structure: the explanation recurses into itself. Used in: tree traversals, divide-and-conquer algorithms (mergesort, quicksort), parser generators, fractal generation, every programming language compiler. Equivalent expressive power to iteration via tail-call optimization (every recursive function can be rewritten as a loop, theoretically).
wisdom: A definition that includes itself, with a stopping rule. CS undergraduates struggle with it until they don't. The proverb is a teaching joke that is also computer-science syntax.

title: Hash tables look up in constant time.
verification: Hash tables: lookup time O(1) average case, O(n) worst case. The average case requires a good hash function (uniform distribution of keys → buckets) and a low load factor (typically <0.75 occupancy). Collisions resolved via chaining or open addressing. Worst case occurs when many keys hash to same bucket — degenerates to linear search. Hash flooding attacks deliberately produce this (HashDoS — found in PHP, Python, JavaScript in 2003-2012). Modern hash tables use cryptographic or randomized hashing to resist this. The proverb is true with caveats. Python dictionaries, Java HashMaps, JavaScript objects, every key-value store: same principle.
wisdom: Cheap when you write it; expensive when an adversary exploits the worst case. The proverb is a sales pitch; the asterisk is on the receipt.

title: Time-and-a-half after 40 hours — FLSA.
verification: Fair Labor Standards Act (1938, US): non-exempt employees must be paid overtime at 1.5× their regular rate for hours worked over 40 in a workweek. Federal minimum wage: $7.25/hour (unchanged since 2009). States set higher floors (CA: $16.00, NY/NJ: $15+). Math example: $20/hour × 50 hours = $20 × 40 + $30 × 10 = $1100 gross. Exempt vs non-exempt status depends on duties test (salary > $35,568 + executive/admin/professional duties = exempt). Federal department of labor enforces. Overtime is one of the most-litigated labor topics; misclassification is a frequent class-action target.
wisdom: The math is unambiguous; the application is famously contested. Misclassifying employees as 'exempt' to dodge overtime is one of the most common wage-and-hour violations. The proverb is the rule; the rule has detail.

title: Most nodes have few connections; a few nodes have many — scale-free network.
verification: Barabási-Albert model (1999): networks growing via preferential attachment ('rich get richer') produce a degree distribution following a power law P(k) ∝ k^(-γ), typically with γ between 2 and 3. Examples: World Wide Web hyperlinks, citation networks, social media followings, airline route networks, protein interaction networks. Implications: networks have hubs (highly-connected nodes) and many low-connection peripheral nodes. Resilient to random failures, vulnerable to targeted attacks on hubs. Different from random (Erdős-Rényi) networks, where degree distribution is binomial/Poisson and there are no hubs. Most real-world networks are scale-free.
wisdom: Twitter, the web, citation rankings — all the same shape. Power-law-distributed networks behave very differently from uniform networks. The proverb is the universal pattern of growth networks.

title: Brace against lateral load — that's what diagonals are for.
verification: Rectangular frame collapses under lateral load (wind, earthquake). Adding a diagonal converts the frame into two triangles → rigid. Bracing methods: cross-brace (X), knee-brace, plywood shear walls, moment-resisting frames. Modern building codes require lateral bracing for all structures over a certain height/area. Engineering math: a frame's lateral stiffness ∝ EI/h³ for moment frames vs ∝ EA·cos²θ for diagonal braces (much stiffer per pound). Earthquake regions (California, Japan, Chile) have stricter lateral bracing codes; tornado regions less so but still required. Failure mode: lateral collapse (pancaking) — the structure remains intact in compression but folds sideways. Most catastrophic earthquake building failures are this.
wisdom: Up-down loads are easy; sideways loads are what kills buildings. The diagonal turns the rectangle into a triangle and the failure mode disappears. Every bridge truss and every modern building has this design rule in it.

title: The circle of fifths organizes 12 keys.
verification: Starting from C and moving in perfect fifths (frequency ratio 3:2): C → G → D → A → E → B → F# → C# (= Db) → Ab → Eb → Bb → F → C. After 12 fifths, you return to C — but in equal temperament. In pure 3:2 fifths, 12 fifths overshoot 7 octaves by the Pythagorean comma (~23.5 cents). Modern Western music adopts equal temperament: each semitone exactly 2^(1/12) ratio, dividing the comma evenly across all keys. This compromise allows modulation between any keys; the cost is that no interval (except octave) is mathematically pure. Bach's Well-Tempered Clavier exploited the system.
wisdom: Twelve fifths almost equal seven octaves. The 'almost' is the Pythagorean comma. Equal temperament smears the error so every key is slightly wrong but uniformly wrong. The trade-off makes harmonic music possible.

title: A above middle C is 440 hertz.
verification: International standard A4 = 440 Hz, formalized by ISO 16:1975. Each octave doubles frequency: A3 = 220 Hz, A5 = 880 Hz. Semitone ratio in equal temperament: 2^(1/12) ≈ 1.0595, so C above (3 semitones higher than A) ≈ 523.25 Hz. Historical pitches varied: Baroque often A415, classical period A430, current European orchestras drift toward A442–446 for brightness, while some early-music ensembles use A415. The proverb names one specific standard, which is the most common one in 2024.
wisdom: Pitch isn't a Platonic fact; it's a convention. 440 Hz won the 20th century; period instruments use earlier conventions. The proverb is true for the standard everyone tunes to.

title: AC won the grid; DC won the device.
verification: Late-19th-century 'War of Currents': Edison's direct current (DC) lost to Tesla/Westinghouse's alternating current (AC) for grid distribution because AC can be transformed up to high voltage (low loss in transmission: P_loss = I²R, so higher voltage = lower current = less loss) and stepped back down at the load. AC has dominated grid distribution since ~1895. Inside devices, however, semiconductors require DC. Modern: HVDC (high-voltage DC) is making a comeback for very-long-distance transmission and submarine cables; renewable inverter technology has matured. The proverb names the historical settlement, which is now mildly under revision.
wisdom: The war ended a century ago, mostly. The grid is AC; your phone is DC; the wall socket has a small power supply doing the conversion. Renewables and long submarine cables are nudging some grids back toward DC.

title: Ground gives current somewhere safe to go.
verification: Electrical grounding (earthing): connecting metal parts of equipment to literal earth (rod driven into ground, or building ground plane). If a fault occurs (live wire touches metal chassis), current flows through ground rather than through a person. National Electrical Code requires grounding on all 120V+ installations. Three-prong outlets: hot, neutral, ground. GFCI (ground-fault circuit interrupter) detects 5+ mA difference between hot and neutral (indicating current leaking somewhere — like through a person) and trips in <0.025 sec, well below cardiac fibrillation threshold (~30 mA). Mandatory in bathrooms, kitchens, outdoors, garages.
wisdom: The third prong isn't decoration. It's the path that takes a fault away from you. GFCIs are why electrocution deaths have fallen ~80% since 1973.

title: The dot product measures alignment.
verification: a · b = |a||b|cos(θ), where θ is the angle between vectors. When θ = 0 (parallel): dot product = |a||b|. When θ = 90° (perpendicular): dot product = 0. When θ = 180° (opposite): dot product = -|a||b|. Foundation of: machine learning (cosine similarity for document retrieval), physics (work = force · displacement), 3D graphics (lighting calculations: brighter when surface normal aligned with light), neural networks (every dense layer is matrix multiplication = many dot products). The dot product is one of mathematics' most useful single operations.
wisdom: Two vectors point the same way → big positive. Two vectors point opposite → big negative. Perpendicular → zero. Most of machine learning is dot products at scale. The proverb is geometry compressed.

title: A determinant is a signed volume.
verification: For an n×n matrix, the determinant equals the signed volume of the parallelepiped spanned by its rows (or columns). 2×2: signed area of parallelogram = ad - bc. 3×3: signed volume of parallelepiped. Sign indicates orientation (handedness). Determinant = 0 means vectors are linearly dependent (collapsed onto a lower-dimensional subspace). Inverse matrix exists iff det ≠ 0. Used in: change-of-variable formulas in integration, eigenvalue computation, Cramer's rule for linear systems, physical conservation (Jacobian determinant in flux calculations). One number captures volume + orientation + invertibility.
wisdom: Three properties (volume, orientation, invertibility) in one number. Linear algebra's most compact diagnostic. Most numerical-method failures trace to a determinant that should be nonzero but isn't.

title: Eigenvectors are the natural axes of a transformation.
verification: For a linear transformation T and eigenvector v: T(v) = λv — T stretches v by factor λ (the eigenvalue) without changing direction. Symmetric matrices have orthogonal eigenvectors (spectral theorem). Foundation of: PCA (principal component analysis: data's eigenvectors of covariance matrix capture directions of most variance), Google's PageRank (web graph's dominant eigenvector ranks pages), quantum mechanics (energy eigenstates), structural engineering (vibration mode analysis). PCA reduces 1000-dimensional data to 2-3 axes for visualization; the math is just eigendecomposition.
wisdom: Find the axes the transformation respects, and the transformation is just stretching along those axes. Google ranked the web with one eigenvector. The proverb is the geometry of every linear system.

title: Every integer has exactly one prime factorization.
verification: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic (proved Euclid, c. 300 BCE): every integer >1 can be written as a product of primes in exactly one way (up to ordering). 12 = 2² × 3. 100 = 2² × 5². 2024 = 2³ × 11 × 23. Underlies modern cryptography (RSA): the difficulty of factoring large semiprimes (product of two large primes) into their factors is what keeps internet banking secure. Best known classical algorithms: number field sieve, sub-exponential. Shor's algorithm (quantum) factors in polynomial time — driving the urgency of post-quantum cryptography. The theorem is mathematical bedrock; cryptography rests on the difficulty of running it backward.
wisdom: Numbers are like molecules: each has a unique prime decomposition. The forward direction (multiplying) is easy; the backward direction (factoring) is hard. RSA encryption survives on the asymmetry. Quantum computers threaten this.

title: Due process — notice, hearing, neutral decision-maker.
verification: Due process doctrine: the right to be informed of charges, to be heard in defense, to have a neutral judge. US Constitution 5th and 14th Amendments. Roots in Magna Carta (1215, clause 39): 'no free man shall be seized... except by the lawful judgment of his peers or by the law of the land.' Deeper roots in Mosaic law (Exodus 23:1-3, 6-8; Deuteronomy 16:18-20 — 'justice, justice shall you pursue'). Every modern legal system formalizes these three elements in some form. Stark deviations (Star Chamber, secret trials, gulags) consistently produce wrongful convictions and political abuse. The proverb is the load-bearing structure of fair adjudication.
wisdom: Three elements, all required. Remove notice and the accused doesn't know how to defend. Remove hearing and the accused can't defend. Remove neutrality and the defense is theater. Most political abuse erodes one of the three.

title: Atoms in equal atoms out — the chemical equation balances.
verification: Conservation of mass (Lavoisier, 1789): in any closed chemical reaction, total mass of reactants equals total mass of products. Operationally: each element's atom count must be equal on both sides of a balanced equation. 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O has 4H + 2O on each side. Charge also conserved in balanced equations. Modern refinement: in nuclear reactions, mass-energy (E=mc²) is conserved, not mass alone — but at chemistry's energy scales, mass conservation holds to better than 10⁻⁹. Foundation of stoichiometry, the calculations every chemistry student learns and every industrial chemist uses daily.
wisdom: The accounting principle of chemistry. Nothing disappears in a chemical reaction; atoms just rearrange. The equation balances or you've described an event the universe doesn't allow.

title: Entropy of an isolated system never decreases.
verification: Second law of thermodynamics. Entropy (Clausius, 1865; Boltzmann statistical reformulation, 1877): in an isolated system, entropy can only increase or stay constant; never spontaneously decrease. Statistical interpretation: a closed system tends toward its most probable macrostate (highest microstate count). The 'arrow of time' — physical processes are reversible in microscopic detail but irreversible in macroscopic effect (an egg unscrambling is statistically impossible, not physically forbidden). Maxwell's demon, Loschmidt's paradox, Poincaré recurrence — debated edge cases that confirm the rule rather than break it. The most-confirmed law in all of physics.
wisdom: Time has a direction because entropy does. Eggs don't unscramble; cups don't unbreak. The proverb is the universe's most reliable one-way street.

title: No heat engine exceeds the Carnot efficiency.
verification: η_max = 1 − T_cold/T_hot (absolute temperatures). Sadi Carnot (1824, only published paper in his lifetime): a theoretical reversible cycle sets the upper bound on heat engine efficiency. Real engines (automobile, jet, steam power) always achieve less because their cycles are irreversible (friction, finite-rate heat transfer). Example: T_hot = 800 K (combustion), T_cold = 300 K (atmosphere) → η_max = 62.5%. Real engines: ~35–45% (gas turbines), ~25–35% (auto), ~38–43% (large coal plants). Trying to exceed Carnot efficiency is impossible — many claimed 'free energy' devices reduce to Carnot violation. The proverb is one of physics's hardest no.
wisdom: Heat engines are bound by the temperature ratio they operate across. The proverb is the speed limit of every car engine, every power plant, every refrigerator (run in reverse). 60% is the ceiling; the rest is detail.

title: At absolute zero, entropy goes to a minimum.
verification: Third law (Nernst, 1906): the entropy of a perfect crystal at absolute zero (0 K = -273.15°C) is exactly zero. Implication: absolute zero cannot be reached in a finite number of steps (would require infinite steps); only approached asymptotically. Current record: nuclear cooling has reached ~10⁻¹⁰ K in laboratory. Practical use: cryogenic technologies (MRI superconducting magnets at 4 K, quantum computers at <100 mK). At 0 K, all thermal motion ceases; matter remains due to quantum zero-point energy. The proverb is the thermodynamic floor.
wisdom: There is a coldest possible temperature. You can get arbitrarily close; you cannot reach it. The proverb is one of the universe's fundamental boundaries.

title: A good hash is a one-way function.
verification: Cryptographic hash properties: 1) preimage resistance (given h(x), hard to find x); 2) second-preimage resistance (given x, hard to find x' with h(x) = h(x')); 3) collision resistance (hard to find any two inputs with same hash). SHA-256: output 256 bits, brute-force preimage ~2²⁵⁶ operations (effectively impossible). MD5 (deprecated): broken by Wang et al. 2004 for collisions. SHA-1 (deprecated): broken 2017 (SHAttered). Modern standards: SHA-256, SHA-3, BLAKE2. Foundation of: password storage (never store plaintext, hash + salt), blockchain (transaction integrity), digital signatures, version control (git uses SHA-1 → SHA-256). The proverb names a one-way function the universe seems to support.
wisdom: Easy to go forward, hard to go backward. Modern security rests on this asymmetry. When hash functions break (MD5, SHA-1), the security collapses; the migration is expensive.

title: Every channel has a maximum information rate.
verification: Shannon-Hartley theorem (1948): the maximum error-free data rate C of a channel with bandwidth B and signal-to-noise ratio S/N is C = B·log₂(1 + S/N) bits per second. Practical implications: doubling SNR adds 1 bit/s/Hz of capacity (logarithmic), while doubling bandwidth doubles capacity (linear). Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, optical fiber, satellite links — all designed against Shannon limits. Modern 5G achieves ~50% of theoretical Shannon for typical channel conditions. Quantum communication can exceed classical Shannon under specific conditions; classical channels obey it strictly.
wisdom: You can't push more information through a channel than its capacity allows. The proverb is engineering's hardest constraint — Shannon proved it in 1948 and every wireless standard has worked toward it since.

title: Compression ratio = original size / compressed size.
verification: Lossless compression of English text: ~3:1 (gzip), ~4:1 (bzip2), ~5:1 (xz/LZMA). Compression of random data: ~1:1 (incompressible). Compression of redundant data (DNA sequence, source code): up to 20:1. Lossy compression (JPEG, MP3, H.264) trades quality for higher ratio — common 10:1 to 100:1 depending on quality setting. Lower bound: Shannon entropy of the source. No algorithm compresses every input losslessly (pigeonhole principle, see map_compression_loss). The proverb is the unit of comparison; the underlying information content is what limits it.
wisdom: How much you can compress depends on how predictable the source is. The proverb is a ratio; the principle is information theory. English compresses well; random data not at all.

title: Soil's infiltration capacity sets the runoff.
verification: Infiltration capacity (Horton equation): rate at which soil absorbs water from the surface. Initial rate high (~50 mm/h for sandy soils, ~10 mm/h for clay), decreases asymptotically to saturated hydraulic conductivity. When rainfall rate > infiltration capacity, excess becomes surface runoff. Paved surfaces have near-zero infiltration → 100% runoff. Forested watersheds have high infiltration → low peak flows. Urban flood mitigation: green roofs, permeable pavement, retention ponds — all engineering infiltration capacity back into otherwise paved areas. The proverb is one of hydrology's foundational equations.
